sentences.text: The first thing is I tried to go home, to Poland, to the place actually, I was picked up inside the camp.
I remember my father used to say, whoever will survive should come in here.
At least we will know who survived and who did not.
When I came-- when I came to Lask, as close that I came to Lask, [? it's ?] [? dark ?] [? there. ?] I mean, I could see the picture.
The picture start getting, there, dark.
Very dark.
I came to the city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I find out that none of my family survived.
Nobody was around.
There were some people, what that came back, maybe about five or six.
When I asked a Polish guy, did any Jews survive?
He said, many of them.
Maybe five are here.
I mean, for them, that was a lot of Jews, you see?
And I remain in the city a certain length of time.
And I haven't seen no future, so I went into Lodz.
And I worked-- after the war-- in Lodz, in a tannery.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I got to know, naturally, my wife, my first wife, Faye, Bless in Memory.
She-- she is from Lask.
She used to live across the street from me, in the ghetto.
But we never had any contact.
You know, she used to live, before the war, in a different vicinity.
You know, the-- the-- their-- their parents belonged to a different religious group than my parents, more Orthodox.
Yes, my wife, yes, was more Orthodox.
And-- and we got to know each other.
And she was sick.
She was sick.
They claimed that she had bone tuberculose.
She got hit on the left shoulders.
But we got married on September 6, 1945, in Lodz.
Rothstein. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her maiden name was [?
Rothstein ?].
She survived with a sister.
The sister lives also in Chicago.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got married.
And we were in Lodz until 1946, or beginning, let's say, 1945, the end of 1945.
We left Poland.
We have seen that there's no place for Jews anymore, in Poland.
And we figured we're going to go to Germany.
And from Germany, that's going to give us a chance of immigrating to different countries.
I didn't have any intention of remaining in Germany.
I didn't hear the-- the question.
In the beginning, I did not know.
In the beginning I did not know.
But by the end of 1945, I find out that one brother passed away and two brothers survived, because somebody came back from Soviet Russia.
Then I find out also later on, in the end of 1945, that my brother survive.
I find somebody, what was liberated.
I find him on the rail-- on the train coming home to Lask, that he survive.
Him, I find out the first.
Then I find out the other brother.
But I didn't know where and when, you see?
Later on, I find out that he is in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I was married already.
September 6, 1945, we got married.
And we decided that-- we-- we left Germany.
We came to a place, Unterammergau, a small village.
But she was sick.
She was constantly sick.
And she was-- she was shipped to a-- a sanatorium with the name Gauting, in Germany, that used to be for German-- for SS of the hierarchy, they used to go for vacation there.
And they used to make a sanatorium for the sick people, what that-- things to do with-- I mean, with sickness for tuberculose.
And she was there for about until 1947.
Then they find out that she got another sickness beside this thing, that they explain the lungs, besides having bone tuberculose, she was treated also, on tuberculose on the lungs.
And she was there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --until 1947.
In 1947, she was operated, in Munich.
She had also-- that start having pain.
The doctor said that this is not tuberculose.
This may be osteomyelitis.
And that should have been operated.
So they were better doctors, I would say.
That was in Munich, in the [INAUDIBLE] Hospital, in Royal Hospital.
And we had the best professors there.
One-- one time, a professor came from [? north, ?] or from Berlin.
And he checked my wife.
And he said that this is osteomyelitis.
This got to be operated.
So they operated on her.
And that closed up.
She had a fistula.
Pus was coming out.
With tuberculose, you could not force this thing, you know, to close it.
Because this will spread all over the body.
In the meantime, this closed up.
In the meantime, she had a pain on the right side, on the back here.
So they took a [? punction. ?] And they find out that this is TB, tuberculose.
So naturally, they assume that both thing are tuberculose.
They opened it up again.
Then I had before, she had one fistula-- pus was coming out.
Now she had from two side.
One from the left arm, and then from the right seat.
And she was in the hospital there, from 1947.
And in 1947, I came to Bayreuth.
And she was also in a big hospital in the city of Bayreuth.
This is in Oberfranken, where Richard Wagner actually composed his music.
And also they find out that she's-- she's sick.
She got [? miliary ?] tuberculose, and contagious.
I couldn't-- she was in an isolated place.
So one time, I took her to a hospital in Erlangen.
That was near that.
Erlangen for Germany is like Rochester for the United States.
And she was checked over.
And she said, no, she-- she got problem with the spleen.
The trouble is with the spleen, not with the lungs.
So they eliminate that the-- before she was laying with-- with tuberculose, with-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do you call-- [? miliary ?] That is [? miliary. ?] It's not on the lungs.
It's not one hole or one spot.
It's a lots of spot.
And the person usually cannot live more than six to eight weeks.
Well, they-- they was proven that they're wrong.
My wife did not die in six weeks, and didn't die in eight weeks.
So I took her to Erlangen.
In Erlangen, they find out that there is nothing wrong with the lungs.
She had-- the-- the spleen was swollen.
And they checked the blood.
And they said that they won't operate on her.
But we said, we-- we can cure you also by trying, giving-- she had treatment.
She got-- radium treatment.
You know?
You get so many minutes per radium.
They put it on the spleen.
And the temperature could not be dissolved.
She had constantly, high fever.
So somebody told me there is an [INAUDIBLE] practice in Nuremberg.
You know, a [INAUDIBLE] practice, this is a-- a person what can cure with no medication.
He only cures with weeds, you know?
Veg, all kind of stuff.
He looks in the eyes, and he tells you.
And I brought the hair from her.
He didn't want to go to the hospital.
So he said the doctor told me that I should bring her hair.
And I brought the hair.
And she said-- the doc-- he find out that-- he said that she is sick.
She got trouble with her blood.
I thought that he is a-- an angel from the sky.
He came down.
You know?
He find out from this.
And he came, he said-- he gave me a shot.
And I took that shot, I took to the hospital.
Naturally, the doctors would not give her a shot, because they didn't know.
So I bribed a nurse.
And the nurse gave the-- used the shot for my wife.
And the-- the fever went away.
For two, three days, the fever went away.
Somebody from the nurses probably tipped off the professor, what took care of my wife.
And he-- he was called down.
They called me down and asked me I should tell them what's happened, and because we know that somebody gave her a shot.
He said, I'm not interested who gave it, the professor said.
I know that we have done lots of bad things to your people.
If he could help you, tell the doctor to come in here.
We have done everything we could.
But we cannot go.
We came to a dead end.
But if you feel that this doctor could help him, call him up.
Tell him to come on here.
So I called him from the doctor's-- from the professor's office.
And I told him if he wanted, I got the permission that he should come.
He didn't want to come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He did not want to come.
Then he-- the-- the professor ask me, is he willing to treat my wife as a patient if I'm going to take her home?
You know, he-- the professor, Professor Mathis was his name.
I took my wife home.
He did some hocus-pocus.
I don't know what he did.
The temperature went away for another couple days.
And everybody find out that he is-- he's Jesus Christ.
I mean, he helps people that nobody could help.
And lots of people came to me.
But I had two friends-- they had a sister-- in Gauting, where my wife was in the beginning.
And she was very sick.
She couldn't come to see him.
So he then start bringing-- I said, I got a suggestion.
Next week, he's going to be there on Friday.
Bring the hair from your sister, and we will see her.
He's going to guess-- he's going to find out what's wrong with her.
So he went to her sister.
He put the-- he put the hair.
He took some hair from her-- from her head.
And he put in an envelope.
And he forgot to bring it home.
So he came on Friday, he came.
And I ask him, did you brought the hair?
He looked at it.
He said, I forgot.
I left it there.
He said, don't worry about.
I'm going to test this guy.
I took some of my hair, put it in an envelope, and I gave it to him.
And I told him, this is the sick sister from the two brothers, what you met last week.
I said, now I got a golden opportunity to find out what he is.
He came out with all kind of sickness.
I got scared stiff.
[LAUGHS] But one sickness he came out with, he said that the patient is blind.
He cured me right away.
[LAUGHS]
And I said, [?
Herr ?] [?
Klaus-- ?] [?
Klaus ?] was his name-- how can you find out from those-- those things?
He said this, they never lie to me.
So I said to him, you know what, doctor?
It looks like my wife feels better.
And we shall say should I need you, I will call you to your number-- Right.
I-- I got your telephone number.
And that was the end of the treatment.
Yes.
Real doctors.
I feel-- I felt pretty good because they were decent, nice people to us.
And they tried everything.
They even tried to look on that she hasn't got a veneric disease.
You know, they were looking for any kinds because the blood didn't-- the blood told them that this is-- she got lympho-- it's in the blood, sickness.
That was a disease in the blood.
And I got even the papers here, that they-- that they decided that this is lymphogranulomatosis.
[INAUDIBLE].
And she-- the doctor said that she's going to die from that.
But those doctors tried to say, we got nothing to lose.
Let's open her up and see.
And if I would listen to them, she would've eliminate a lot of problems.
I find this thing out later, but you see?
But how much did you know about these doctors?
Did you know whether or not they were former members of the Nazi party?
Did you know--
No.
We did know that they are most responsible people working in those hospitals, that there was a-- a university, like-- like here in now, Rochester.
They had good doctors, professors.
A woman doctor was the one what find out that her lungs, there is nothing wrong.
She is treated wrong-- wrong on the lungs.
You see, but we did not know who is good and who's bad.
But you could only trust if you go in in a city hospital.
Like, you know.
Or you go in, let's say, to a hospital in Erlangen.
You know, you got professional people.
Yeah, I could have gone into a  doctor, a DP doctor.
Now, I don't know who the-- The Jewish doctors were-- were killed.
So we were depending on either Russian, Ukrainian.
But for them, I didn't trust at all.
I trusted more the-- the German doctors in the hospitals than trusting people like, you know, from other nationalities.
Because if he would be a good doctor and wouldn't have-- would have a good-- he had no business being in Germany.
He would go back to his place where he was born and raised.
You know?
That's interesting.
I mean-- I mean, this is a logical approach.
I also had help from now, the-- the UNRRA helped me make the arrangement that she should go to Erlangen.
They tried to accommodate me as much as they could.
You know?
That was help from the United State.
They made arrangements to go to Erlangen.
As a matter of fact, the doctor said that let's try to open her up, see what the spleen is.
Because the spleen was swollen.
So how long did you remain in Europe?
At what point did you decide to come to the United States?
I came in-- in the beginning of '46 or end of '45, to Germany.
I know I got no chance going anywhere else, because on account of my wife.
And I would not leave her.
Because she was sick.
She was in the hospital.
And I wouldn't go without my wife.
So I know that at one time I went into a camp near Munich.
And I have seen a line.
I ask the people, what is that line?
I said, this is a registration to the United State.
So you know, a line like this didn't interest me because I got no chance coming to the United States.
My wife, she is-- she's laying in a hospital.
At that time, she was in [INAUDIBLE], also in a convalescent home, with old people.
Coming home, I was looking to get liver for her.
Because they said for blood sickness it's good to-- to eat liver.
So coming home, I have seen the line shrink to a few people.
So I stopped.
I got off the streetcar.
And I figure, I got nothing to lose.
They register to the United States, I'll register too.
But in the meantime, my wife wasn't on-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] My wife, she was in a hospital in [INAUDIBLE].
After she had the treatment, the doctor-- my wife didn't want to go through the treatment.
Because somebody told her she will never be able to bear children.
So she put a stop to the treatment, to the radiation-- the radium treatment.
Because on the spleen, that will burn all the-- you know, that a woman could not bear any more children.
Then she said stop.
And the professor said, you won't be able to have-- bear any more children.
You have to worry to have a mother yourself.
Now you're thinking about being a mother?
And she said, no.
If I cannot bear any children, then I don't want the treatment.
And she stopped with the treatment.
I-- I could not convince her to go through with the treatment.
And the doctor said that she can never bear any children, because she had seven, eight treatments already.
You know, you go every minute-- every time you go for more minutes, you know?
And she had almost all-- she was almost through with the treatment.
And she said no.
Now, we went into a doctor.
I took my wife to a doctor in-- in [INAUDIBLE].
And this was an old-time doctor.
And he asked her if she received quinine.
And the doctor-- she said, yes, I did receive quinine.
But I got sick from it and I had to throw it out.
So what he did is try to put the quinine into-- through a tube, through the stomach, into the intestine.
And for one or another reason, the temperature disappeared, and she start feeling better.
So at that point, you felt comfortable to--
No, no.
I mean, she was still in the old people home.
She was old people.
But it was Passover in 1949.
And we decided that we're going to go home.
You know, I was living far away.
I was living not far from Bayreuth.
And we decided-- I mean, we know that we're going to be together for Passover.
Because well, I was-- during the five years, we were never together.
So we know that she can never bear any Children so I didn't try to protect anything.
You know?
And she came back to the hospital.
She find out that she is pregnant.
And all of a sudden, all the fistulas all closed on her.
You know, she had open wounds in here and in the-- the back.
And out of nowhere, the whole thing closed.
And three weeks later, we find out that she is pregnant.
I mean, she's pregnant.
I mean, the doctor said that we cannot have any patients in an old people home with pregnant woman.
She said, I mean, this is not the place for you.
And she said, she got to go through an abortion.
And I remember, she was laying in a hospital bed.
And next to her was laying another, it was three feet apart, an elderly woman.
She was from Chelm.
You know, the city of Chelm, they used-- the smart people used to come.
And that old woman said to my wife, do you know, Mrs.
Toporek, what you are doing?
Do you know the crime?
All the geniuses said that you cannot become pregnant, and you became pregnant.
This is a-- a thing from--
God wants you to be a mother.
Don't do it.
There is no crime.
There is no punishment for doing something, what you want to do.
So and I remember, the doctor came.
And he had the shot.
And she said no.
If this is what's happened to me, let it go.
Let nature take its course.
And she had to leave the hospital.
She start feeling good.
But the professor said there is no place.
And the doctor said that a child should be born, and the spleen is so big, there is no place for two.
Either you're going to have a dead body-- a child, or you're going to lose your life.
Or something going to happen.
But she felt good.
She felt excellent.
For one or another reason, looks like it-- like the thing shrinked.
When she start feeling-- during the time when she start feeling better, I got papers to come to the United State.
So we only answer question to the United State, what they ask us.
I didn't tell it that she was sick of tuberculose, or she-- she's still in the hospital.
I-- you know, I could have a different address.
And we came to the United State.
And I was worry about, you know, that-- that we were-- in Germany, I know this is not the place.
In the United States, maybe I will be able to have help.
We came here in September-- in October the 13.
On January the 5th, my son, Jack, was born in Mount Sinai Hospital, a handsome, beautiful boy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came-- we took Jack to the doctor, what advised us not to have any-- any children, because-- [?
Berman. ?] And he said, if you were lucky once, don't try it again.
[LAUGHTER]
You know?
If somebody jumps from the third floor and doesn't break his legs, he--
[LAUGHS] That doesn't mean that he should do it again.
And we show him.
You see, this is our grandpa, we used to tell him.
He was an elderly man.
He actually gave her the quinine through the-- in through the stomach, into the intestine.
And this actually subdued the heat-- the temperature.
And she start feeling great.
And we came to the United State.
She wasn't sick.
She was not a-- a healthy woman.
But you know, she brought three kids in this world, three lovely kids.
I mean, she was pregnant.
She was expecting a baby.
I have seen-- this is a place of opportunity.
I start working.
I got-- through the HIAS, I got a job in the tannery.
I worked here.
And I made $60 a week.
That was a lots of money.
No other people made $60 at that time.
I mean, very seldom.
People used to work for $20 and $30 dollars a week.
And we were so happy.
We had a healthy child.
And my wife, we were the happiest people in the world.
In Chicago.
In Chicago.
Jack was born in Mount Sinai Hospital in Chicago.
I used to live at Springfield Avenue, 5036 Springfield.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then she-- she gave birth to two more kids.
Thanks God, I mean.
But all my years with her, I was living in fear that something shouldn't pop out.
But I didn't expect that she's going to die.
She's-- you know, that she's going to die on complete different things, that she's going to have a brain hemorrhage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, she-- this-- well, this-- this came out of nowhere.
She was pregnant.
You didn't expect this to happen.
You're in a new land, a lot of hope.
People what know me, they know the story.
People what didn't know, didn't care.
No, no.
No, no, no.
No, I mean, you could not constantly-- and I did not want to discuss this thing with my wife.
Because she was depending on my health.
And I didn't want to talk about it.
You know?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She did more talking to the kids than I did.
You know, I didn't want to say, I married a sick girl.
Or you know, if that wouldn't be for me, maybe, we-- no.
I didn't.
We-- we never talked about it.
Oh, my kids were aware about the Holocaust, as little as the war.
As a matter of fact, when Debbie, the youngest, who is 35, she once had watched television.
And she have seen they rind up-- round up-- the Germans round up people in-- in-- France, I think.
And she came in, and she-- mommy, it's a good thing that the Germans didn't-- didn't kill you.
If not, I would have to have a stepmother.
She was a baby.
She was two years old.
She came with a statement like this.
So she heard about it.
You know, the-- the kids actually were brought up with this thing.
Because we both were survivors.
And we talked about people, you see?
About the sickness, my wife, their mother told them more than I did.
Whatever they know about the relationship between me, when we got married, and about the sickness, came from the-- from my wife, what she told them.
Yes.
Yes.
When I came in here-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was [? ruptured ?] in Europe.
I had, you know, a hernia.
And I got in concentration camp, in the camp, and I have realized that this came back in the camp.
So I used to-- I used to go in the camp with a-- a girl, you know, that should hold this thing.
But I have seen if they're going to find that girl, they're going to send me away.
So I throw this thing away.
And that was in 1942.
And this thing didn't came out-- I was working the coal mines.
And the hernia did not came out until I came to the United States and I was here in Chicago.
The hernia came back.
So they took me in the hospital, to Mount Sinai Hospital.
And they operated on me.
And the doctor looked at this.
He was a-- a child from parents, what they come from my vicinity.
And he said, why do you have to have that number?
Why do you have to look at it?
Why should this be a reminder?
You will remember this thing anyhow.
And he was the surgeon.
So he said, let me take it off.
And you know, I used to play with Jack.
Jack was a baby at that time.
Jack was a baby at that time.
And he used to pull the hair.
You know?
He was fascinated by the number.
So I decided, if he want to take it off-- I remember he just took it off.
Took him about a half an hour.
He cut it, cut it out, [? they ?] [? meet, ?] and put [? the ?] [? meet ?] back together.
And he said that it's gone for good and forever.
And I made a mistake.
I realize now I made a mistake.
I mean, because today we got four consecutive numbers, three here in-- in the city of Chicago.
It's fantastic.
That I removed my number, but I got people one number above me and one number ahead of me and below.
You know?
And this guy, what got a number above my brother's name, he-- his name was not-- he lives in Chicago.
His name was [?
Shantovsky ?] with a K.
But he had a friend, what his name was [?
Tensir. ?]
So he changed his name from K, [?
Shantovksy ?] to [?
Chintovksy ?] to be with him together.
Usually, we liked-- this-- that friend.
And that's the reason he got one number ahead.
Otherwise, he was not entitled for that number.
But I remember the way he pushed himself in.
He didn't want to stay with them together.
So four people, one-- four numbers, consecutive [? in the-- ?] And I made a mistake by removing it.
